stories.headline: Sara Duterte defense files comment as impeachment court prepares pretrial order; trial set for July 6
stories.summary: Vice President Sara Duterte's defense team said it filed its comment on a House prosecution motion before Friday's deadline, responding to requests that include unsealing a Bureau of Internal Revenue box containing her tax records and setting disclosure rules for surprise witnesses. The Senate impeachment court is expected to issue its pretrial order by June 29, with parties given until July 1 to comment. The Senate secretary said the decision on whether to open the BIR box rests with the impeachment court itself, not the pretrial process. The trial proper is scheduled to begin July 6.
stories.context: The five-day pretrial conference for Duterte's Senate impeachment concluded June 25. She faces charges including culpable violation of the Constitution, betrayal of public trust, and questions over confidential funds.
stories.watch_for: The impeachment court is expected to issue its pretrial order by June 29; the trial proper begins July 6, 2026.

stories.headline: House prosecutors keep alleged fictitious 'Mary Grace Piattos' on impeachment witness list
stories.summary: House prosecutors confirmed they listed 'Mary Grace Piattos' as a witness in the Duterte impeachment trial even though they believe the person does not exist. Prosecution spokespersons said listing the name gives anyone using it a chance to come forward and lets the court establish the person's identity. The Philippine Statistics Authority found no birth, marriage, or death record matching the name, which appears on confidential-fund acknowledgment receipts from the Office of the Vice President and the Department of Education.
stories.context: The use of OVP and DepEd confidential funds is central to the impeachment case. The name resembles a snack brand, fueling allegations it was fabricated to liquidate funds.
stories.watch_for: Whether anyone appears claiming the identity once the trial proper begins July 6, 2026.

stories.headline: NBI says strong indication Senator Dela Rosa is still in the Philippines as ICC warrant manhunt continues
stories.summary: National Bureau of Investigation Director Melvin Matibag said there is a strong indication that Senator Ronald 'Bato' dela Rosa remains in the country, citing the difficulty of leaving given the International Criminal Court's wide membership. Dela Rosa, sought on an ICC arrest warrant over alleged crimes against humanity tied to the Duterte-era drug war, disappeared from Senate protective custody on May 14 after a shooting incident. Senator Robinhood Padilla said Dela Rosa had asked for a ride from the Senate and was dropped off in Makati.
stories.context: Dela Rosa, a former national police chief, is wanted by the ICC over killings during the anti-drug campaign he helped lead. Authorities attempted to serve the warrant in May before he went into hiding.
stories.watch_for: The continuing NBI effort to locate Dela Rosa and any move toward his arrest or surrender.

stories.headline: Sandiganbayan says ex-Energy chief Cusi was sufficiently informed of Malampaya graft charges
stories.summary: The Sandiganbayan's Third Division ruled that former Energy Secretary Alfonso Cusi and his co-accused were sufficiently informed of the graft charges they face over the 2019 sale of Chevron's 45 percent stake in the Malampaya gas project to a company linked to businessman Dennis Uy. The court said prosecutors adequately alleged injury to the government, which they estimate at about 177 million US dollars, claiming the stake was undervalued. The accused had earlier posted bail.
stories.context: Malampaya supplies a significant share of the country's natural gas. Prosecutors allege the government approved a disadvantageous sale of the Chevron stake.
stories.watch_for: Arraignment is rescheduled for July 10, 2026; motions for reconsideration may be filed until July 1.

stories.headline: BIR files over P1.68 billion in tax evasion complaints against ex-DPWH officials tied to flood-control probe
stories.summary: The Bureau of Internal Revenue said it secured Department of Justice approval to file criminal tax evasion complaints against three former Department of Public Works and Highways personnel, citing combined liabilities exceeding 1.68 billion pesos from non-filing and alleged underreporting of income. The BIR named Jaypee Mendoza, Henry Alcantara, and Brice Ericson Hernandez, computing their liabilities using the expenditure method. The three are also facing graft complaints before the Ombudsman, and their bank accounts and insurance policies were earlier frozen.
stories.context: The complaints are part of a wider investigation into anomalous flood-control projects involving former DPWH officials.
stories.watch_for: Criminal informations may now be filed before the Court of Tax Appeals; the Ombudsman graft complaints remain pending.

stories.headline: Two minor suspects in Tacloban school shooting held in separate rooms at DSWD facility
stories.summary: The two minors accused of carrying out the June 22 shooting at San Jose National High School in Tacloban, which killed three students and wounded about 20, are being held in separate rooms at a Department of Social Welfare and Development facility in Tanauan, Leyte. Regional Director Grace Subong said the boys, aged 14 and 15, receive no special treatment, have restricted movement, cannot freely use phones or watch television, and that relatives' visits are temporarily barred during assessments. She said they sometimes appeared withdrawn.
stories.context: Because the suspects are below the age of criminal liability, they are held in a DSWD rehabilitation facility rather than detained. The shooting prompted national debate over campus security and violent online games.
stories.watch_for: Ongoing psychological assessments of the two suspects.

stories.headline: GoreBox developer declines Senate invitation as lawmakers probe violent games after Tacloban shooting
stories.summary: Germany-based independent developer Felix Filip, whose game GoreBox authorities have linked to a suspect in the Tacloban school shooting, declined a Senate committee invitation to appear at a July 1 hearing in person or by video. In an email conveying condolences, he noted the game is rated for adults and not intended for minors. Separately, national police chief Gen. Jose Melencio Nartatez Jr. said the force supports a government review of violent video games over concerns they could normalize violence, while stressing any restrictions should be evaluated by Congress based on research.
stories.context: Authorities ordered GoreBox temporarily blocked after linking it to the Tacloban shooting and an earlier foiled attack in Leyte. The Senate inquiry, led by Senator Risa Hontiveros, is examining online platforms and youth radicalization.
stories.watch_for: The Senate committee hearing on the shooting is scheduled for July 1, 2026.

stories.headline: Police investigators recommend hazing charges against ex-Ateneo coach Baldwin and 10 others over players' deaths
stories.summary: The PNP Criminal Investigation and Detection Group recommended filing Anti-Hazing Act charges against 11 people, including former Ateneo de Manila head basketball coach Tab Baldwin, over the June 8 drowning deaths of players Rene Baterbonia and Divine Adili during a training activity in Dipaculao, Aurora. Investigators said the activity began before dawn with a long run and included seawater training in the early afternoon amid rip currents and strong waves, and that none of those present intervened. The Interior Secretary said the activity crossed into hazing.
stories.context: The two Ateneo players drowned during an off-season team activity; Baldwin resigned as head coach. The investigation gathered hundreds of pages of testimony, and officials said former players described a near-drowning at an earlier camp.
stories.watch_for: The Department of Justice will review the recommendation and decide whether to prosecute.

stories.headline: Budget department proposes P7.2-trillion national budget for 2027
stories.summary: The Department of Budget and Management proposed a 7.2-trillion-peso national budget for 2027, about 6 percent higher than the 2026 budget and equal to roughly 21.7 percent of gross domestic product. The department projected economic growth of 5 to 6 percent in 2027 and said agencies must demonstrate readiness to implement projects before receiving funds. It flagged risks including higher local oil prices linked to conflict in the Middle East and corruption concerns surrounding flood-control projects.
stories.context: The memorandum sets the fiscal framework for the 2027 budget that will be submitted to Congress.
stories.watch_for: Submission of the 2027 General Appropriations Bill to Congress and subsequent budget deliberations.

stories.headline: National government budget deficit widened to P198.5 billion in May
stories.summary: The Bureau of the Treasury reported that the national government's budget deficit widened by about 37 percent year on year to 198.5 billion pesos in May, swinging from a surplus the previous month. The Treasury said total revenue collections fell 7.2 percent, driven mainly by a sharp drop in non-tax revenue, which it attributed to government-owned corporations remitting dividends earlier in the year and to one-off receipts in May 2025. Tax collections still grew 12.2 percent while spending rose 3.8 percent.
stories.context: The Treasury described the wider deficit largely as a timing effect from the early remittance of state-firm dividends rather than weaker underlying revenue.

stories.headline: Fitch lowers Philippine banking sector outlook, citing fallout from Middle East conflict
stories.summary: Fitch Ratings said Philippine banks could face higher asset-quality risks and lower profitability in the near term because of economic headwinds from the Middle East conflict, including disruptions to public investment and elevated energy prices. The agency flagged rapid growth in unsecured consumer lending as a vulnerability and said it expects higher provisions to weigh on earnings before a gradual recovery in 2027. Fitch cut its outlook on the sector to deteriorating from neutral this month, after lowering its outlook on the sovereign rating in April.
stories.context: An oil-price shock and slower public spending have weighed on assessments of the Philippine economy in recent months.

stories.headline: US development agency approves $60-million grant for Philippine energy reforms
stories.summary: Finance Secretary Frederick Go said the US Millennium Challenge Corporation board approved a 60-million-dollar threshold program for the Philippines, with US congressional approval expected by August and a signing targeted for September. The grant is intended to support modernization of the energy sector and its regulatory framework, and officials described it as a step toward a larger future compact. The corporation's board chair said the program would strengthen energy security and encourage private investment.
stories.context: The board approved the program on June 24. An earlier Millennium Challenge Corporation compact with the Philippines ran from 2011 to 2016 and was worth 434 million US dollars.
stories.watch_for: US congressional approval is expected by August 2026, with an agreement signing targeted for September 2026.

stories.headline: Magnitude 6.6 earthquake strikes waters off Davao Occidental
stories.summary: A magnitude 6.6 earthquake struck off Davao Occidental at about 7:34 p.m. on June 26, located roughly 90 kilometers southwest of Balut Island in Sarangani at a depth of 10 kilometers, according to the Philippine Institute of Volcanology and Seismology. The strongest shaking, Intensity V, was reported in parts of Sarangani, with weaker shaking felt across Davao City and nearby provinces. Phivolcs said it had not yet confirmed whether the quake was an aftershock of a stronger earthquake that struck the area on June 8. No casualties were reported in the initial account.
stories.context: Southern Mindanao has seen heightened seismic activity, including a stronger offshore earthquake on June 8 that prompted a tsunami warning.
stories.watch_for: Phivolcs is expected to confirm whether the quake is an aftershock and to monitor for further aftershocks.

stories.headline: Phivolcs monitors month-long earthquake swarm off Pangasinan with nearly 290 recorded quakes
stories.summary: The Philippine Institute of Volcanology and Seismology said an earthquake swarm offshore of Bolinao, Pangasinan, that began on May 27 had produced 287 recorded earthquakes as of the morning of June 26. The quakes ranged in magnitude from 1.8 to 5.1, and only one was felt. Phivolcs said a swarm is a cluster of earthquakes without a clear main shock, which may continue without necessarily leading to a stronger event, and advised residents to stay prepared.
stories.context: An earlier count recorded about 104 quakes in the same swarm from late May to early June.
stories.watch_for: Continued Phivolcs monitoring of whether the swarm intensifies.

stories.headline: Monsoon rains and gale warnings persist over Luzon as no tropical cyclone remains in PH area
stories.summary: The state weather bureau PAGASA said the southwest monsoon was bringing rains over much of Luzon and the Visayas on June 26, with a yellow rainfall warning raised over Zambales, Bataan, and Occidental Mindoro and gale-force winds possible over parts of northern and western Luzon. PAGASA said Severe Tropical Storm Gardo had exited the Philippine area of responsibility after a stay of less than a day, heading toward southern Japan, leaving no tropical cyclone within the monitoring area following the earlier departure of another storm.
stories.context: Gardo, the country's seventh tropical cyclone of 2026, stayed far offshore and did not directly affect land. The enhanced monsoon is now the main weather hazard.
stories.watch_for: PAGASA issues updated daily forecasts and rainfall and gale warnings as monsoon conditions evolve over Luzon.

stories.headline: Marcos inspects Quezon City kidney institute project, citing target completion by end of 2027
stories.summary: President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. inspected the under-construction Legacy Building of the National Kidney and Transplant Institute in Quezon City, describing it as a future large dialysis center for the region. Officials said the 13-storey facility could serve more than 200 patients with 200 machines and that the president ordered its completion by the end of 2027. The Public Works department said construction began in 2023 and that the needed 2.8 billion pesos in funding would be completed next year.
stories.context: The institute, established in 1981, treats patients with chronic kidney disease. The project is among the administration's flagship specialty-hospital efforts.
stories.watch_for: The president directed completion of the building by the end of 2027, with full funding targeted next year.

stories.headline: Laborer killed in Negros Occidental after drinking-session dispute; three charged with murder
stories.summary: Police in La Castellana, Negros Occidental, said a 48-year-old laborer was killed on June 24 after a dispute during a drinking session, and that his body was later found in a creek. Authorities said three suspects were implicated, with one identified as the alleged mastermind, and that murder charges were filed on June 25. According to police, one of the suspects admitted involvement in the assault.
stories.watch_for: Arraignment and prosecution of the three suspects on murder charges.

stories.headline: Pew survey finds Filipinos most confident in Trump among 36 countries
stories.summary: A Pew Research Center survey released June 23 found that 68 percent of Filipino adults expressed confidence in US President Donald Trump to do the right thing in world affairs, the highest share among 36 countries surveyed, against a global median of 23 percent. The survey also found 56 percent of Filipinos held a favorable view of the United States, well above the global median, and that majorities saw the US as contributing to global stability.
stories.context: The findings reflect continued strong pro-US sentiment in the Philippines amid a broader global decline in confidence in US leadership.

stories.headline: Venezuela earthquake death toll passes 230 as international rescue teams arrive
stories.summary: Rescue efforts continued in Venezuela after two powerful earthquakes struck on June 25, with Al Jazeera reporting the death toll had risen to at least 235 and more than 4,300 people injured, citing Venezuelan officials. The coastal state of La Guaira was among the hardest hit, with scores of buildings collapsed and its main international airport closed by damage. Several countries and the United Nations dispatched rescue teams and aid, and authorities said hundreds of people remained trapped under rubble.
stories.context: The quakes were among the strongest to hit Venezuela in over a century, striking a country already facing economic and infrastructure strains. The US Geological Survey has cautioned the final toll could be far higher.
stories.watch_for: Officials expect the death toll to rise as search-and-rescue operations continue.

stories.headline: China defends naval patrols east of Taiwan, citing Japan-Philippines maritime boundary talks
stories.summary: China defended recent patrols in waters east of Taiwan, with a Foreign Ministry spokesperson describing them as necessary actions in response to what Beijing called manipulation of maritime boundary issues by Japan and the Philippines. The statement came after Britain, France, and Germany expressed alarm at the activity, which they said threatened regional stability and freedom of navigation, and after Manila and Tokyo announced plans to discuss their maritime boundaries in waters China claims. The United States also voiced concern, according to Taiwanese media.
stories.context: The waters are a major shipping route for energy and goods bound for East Asia. China claims self-governing Taiwan as its territory, and tensions with Japan have risen over comments about possible Japanese military involvement if China attacks Taiwan.
stories.watch_for: Planned Japan-Philippines maritime boundary discussions and any further Chinese coast guard deployments.

stories.headline: Study attributes Europe's record June heat to climate change
stories.summary: A rapid analysis by the World Weather Attribution network concluded that Europe's June 2026 heatwave is the most severe on record for the month and would have been virtually impossible decades ago without human-caused climate change. The group said about 45 percent of the roughly 850 European cities it analyzed had broken or were expected to break heat-stress records, as daytime temperatures exceeded 40 degrees Celsius across much of the continent. Spain reported scores of heat-related deaths, and the heat was forecast to spread eastward.
stories.context: World Weather Attribution is a network of scientists that runs rapid analyses to assess the role of climate change in extreme weather events.
stories.watch_for: Forecasters said the heatwave was moving toward Germany and central Europe, with dangerous conditions expected to continue.

stories.headline: Asian markets slide as tech sell-off triggers circuit breaker on South Korea's Kospi
stories.summary: Asian stock markets fell sharply on June 26, led by a sell-off in technology shares, with trading on South Korea's Kospi briefly halted by a circuit breaker after the index dropped about 8 percent before closing 5.8 percent lower. The BBC reported the decline followed a fall in Apple shares after the company said it would raise prices on some products due to higher chip costs, and amid concern over heavy spending on artificial-intelligence infrastructure. An analyst said investors were becoming more selective about which companies justified their valuations.
stories.context: The drop followed a months-long rally in technology and AI-related stocks that has prompted debate over whether valuations had risen too far.
stories.watch_for: Whether the tech-led sell-off spreads to other markets in the coming sessions.

stories.headline: Western officials warn Russia may stage a provocation against Baltic states or Poland
stories.summary: The Guardian reported that Western sources believe Russia may be preparing a provocation against the Baltic states or Poland to test the cohesion of the NATO alliance, while stopping short of a full-scale attack. Latvian intelligence said it had indications Russia could consider hybrid actions such as missile or drone strikes to pressure allies into reducing support for Ukraine. Following a summit in Gdansk, Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk described the situation as very unstable and said various forms of escalation could be expected in the coming months.
stories.context: The warnings come as Russia's advance in Ukraine has stalled and it faces long-range Ukrainian strikes. Analysts noted the assessments lacked the detailed supporting evidence seen before Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
stories.watch_for: Western governments are watching for possible hybrid actions against NATO's eastern flank in the coming weeks and months.
